antelope.protection_details: Protected in national parks across Africa and India
antelope.fun_fact: Antelopes can run up to 95 km/h making them one of the fastest land animals

badger.protection_details: Protected under the Protection of Badgers Act in the UK
badger.fun_fact: Badgers are incredibly clean animals and never use the toilet inside their burrows

bat.protection_details: Many bat species protected under wildlife acts globally
bat.fun_fact: Bats are the only mammals capable of sustained flight and use echolocation to navigate in complete darkness

bear.fun_fact: Bears can smell food from up to 20 miles away, making their sense of smell 7 times stronger than a bloodhound

bee.protection_details: Protected in several countries due to critical role in ecosystems
bee.fun_fact: A single bee will produce only 1/12 of a teaspoon of honey in its entire lifetime

beetle.habitat: Every habitat except marine and polar
beetle.fun_fact: Beetles make up 25% of all animal species on Earth — one in every four animals is a beetle

bison.protection_details: Protected in national parks including Yellowstone
bison.fun_fact: American bison nearly went extinct in the 1800s when populations fell from 60 million to just 541 animals

boar.protection_details: Hunted in many regions as game animal
boar.fun_fact: Wild boars can dig up to 8 cm deep with their snouts while foraging and can uproot large areas of ground

butterfly.fun_fact: Butterflies taste with their feet — they have taste sensors on their legs to identify plants

cat.protection_details: Protected under animal welfare laws in most countries
cat.fun_fact: Cats spend 70% of their lives sleeping and have a special collarbone that allows them to always land on their feet

caterpillar.fun_fact: A caterpillar multiplies its body mass by 1000 times during its larval stage before becoming a butterfly

chimpanzee.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix I — international trade banned
chimpanzee.fun_fact: Chimpanzees share 98.7% of their DNA with humans and are our closest living relatives

cockroach.fun_fact: Cockroaches can survive without their head for up to one week and can hold their breath for 40 minutes

cow.protection_details: Protected under animal welfare laws; sacred in some cultures
cow.fun_fact: Cows have best friends and become stressed when separated from them — they also have almost 360 degree panoramic vision

coyote.protection_details: Not federally protected in most US states
coyote.fun_fact: Coyotes can run at speeds of up to 65 km/h and are one of the few wild animals expanding their range despite human development

crab.protection_details: Some species regulated by fishing laws
crab.fun_fact: Crabs communicate by drumming or waving their claws and can walk in all four directions

crow.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act in the US
crow.fun_fact: Crows are one of the most intelligent animals and can recognize human faces, use tools, and even hold grudges

deer.fun_fact: Male deer grow and shed their antlers every year — antlers are the fastest growing tissue in the animal kingdom

dog.protection_details: Protected under animal welfare laws in most countries
dog.fun_fact: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more powerful than humans and can detect certain diseases including cancer

dolphin.protection_details: Protected under Marine Mammal Protection Act and CITES
dolphin.fun_fact: Dolphins sleep with one eye open and only half their brain at a time — the other half stays alert for danger

donkey.fun_fact: Donkeys have an incredible memory and can recognize areas and other donkeys they met up to 25 years ago

dragonfly.fun_fact: Dragonflies catch 95% of the prey they chase making them the most successful hunters in the animal kingdom

duck.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
duck.fun_fact: Duck feathers are so waterproof that even after a duck dives underwater the feathers next to its skin stay dry

eagle.protection_details: Protected under Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act
eagle.fun_fact: Eagles can see up to 8 times more clearly than humans and can spot a rabbit from nearly 3 km away

elephant.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix I — ivory trade banned
elephant.fun_fact: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump and are one of the few animals that can recognize themselves in a mirror

flamingo.fun_fact: Flamingos are not born pink — they are born white and turn pink from the pigments in the algae and shrimp they eat

fly.fun_fact: A housefly tastes food using sensors on its feet and can detect sugar at concentrations 10 million times lower than humans

fox.fun_fact: Foxes use the Earth's magnetic field like a compass to help them pounce accurately on prey hidden under snow

goat.fun_fact: Goats have rectangular pupils giving them a 320 degree field of vision and they were one of the first animals domesticated by humans

goldfish.fun_fact: Goldfish do not have a 3-second memory — they can remember things for months and can be trained to navigate mazes

goose.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
goose.fun_fact: Geese mate for life and if one goose is injured the other will often stay behind rather than migrate with the flock

gorilla.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix I — highest protection level
gorilla.fun_fact: Gorillas share 98.3% of their DNA with humans and can learn sign language to communicate with people

grasshopper.fun_fact: Grasshoppers can jump 20 times their body length — equivalent to a human jumping 40 metres in one leap

hamster.protection_details: European hamster protected under EU Habitats Directive
hamster.fun_fact: Hamsters can carry up to half their body weight in food in their cheek pouches and run up to 8 km in a single night

hare.fun_fact: Hares are born with their eyes open and fully furred unlike rabbits and can run at speeds of up to 72 km/h

hedgehog.protection_details: Protected under Wildlife and Countryside Act in UK
hedgehog.fun_fact: A hedgehog has between 5,000 and 7,000 spines and when threatened can roll into a perfect ball for protection

hippopotamus.fun_fact: Despite their bulk hippos are surprisingly agile and can run at 30 km/h on land — faster than most humans

hornbill.protection_details: Protected under wildlife laws in range countries
hornbill.fun_fact: Female hornbills seal themselves inside a tree hollow using mud while nesting — the male feeds her through a tiny slit

horse.protection_details: Protected under animal welfare laws worldwide
horse.fun_fact: Horses can sleep both standing up and lying down and they have the largest eyes of any land mammal

hummingbird.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
hummingbird.fun_fact: Hummingbirds beat their wings 50-80 times per second and are the only birds that can fly backwards

hyena.protection_details: Protected in national parks and game reserves
hyena.fun_fact: Hyenas are not related to dogs — they are more closely related to cats and their bite is the strongest of any land mammal relative to size

jellyfish.habitat: All ocean zones from surface to deep sea
jellyfish.fun_fact: One species, the Turritopsis dohrnii, is considered biologically immortal as it can revert to its juvenile form after reaching maturity

kangaroo.protection_details: Protected under Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act
kangaroo.fun_fact: Kangaroos cannot walk backwards and a group of kangaroos is called a mob — newborn joeys are the size of a grape

koala.protection_details: Protected under Australian law — listed as vulnerable since 2022
koala.fun_fact: Koalas sleep up to 22 hours a day because their eucalyptus diet is so low in nutrition it barely provides enough energy

ladybugs.fun_fact: A ladybug can eat up to 5,000 aphids in its lifetime — making it one of the most beneficial insects for farmers

leopard.fun_fact: Leopards are incredibly strong and can carry prey twice their own body weight up into trees to keep it safe from scavengers

lion.protection_details: Protected under Wildlife Protection Acts and CITES Appendix II
lion.fun_fact: Lions are the only cats that live in groups called prides and a lion's roar can be heard up to 8 km away

lizard.fun_fact: Some lizards can detach their tail when threatened — the tail grows back although it is made of cartilage not bone

lobster.protection_details: Fishing regulated by size and season
lobster.fun_fact: Lobsters do not age in the traditional sense — older lobsters are often more fertile than younger ones and they keep growing throughout their life

mosquito.fun_fact: Mosquitoes are the deadliest animal on Earth responsible for more human deaths annually than any other creature through disease transmission

moth.fun_fact: Moths navigate using moonlight as a compass which is why artificial lights confuse them — they are important nocturnal pollinators

mouse.fun_fact: Mice are highly social animals that feel empathy — they have been shown to feel distress when they see other mice in pain

octopus.fun_fact: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and nine brains — a central brain plus one in each of their eight arms

okapi.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix II and Congolese law
okapi.fun_fact: The okapi was unknown to science until 1901 and despite its striped legs it is more closely related to giraffes than zebras

orangutan.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix I — deforestation is primary threat
orangutan.fun_fact: Orangutans share 96.9% of DNA with humans and are the most intelligent non-human primates — they have been observed making their own medicines from plants

otter.protection_details: Protected under Wildlife and Countryside Act and similar laws
otter.fun_fact: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart and they are one of the few animals that use tools — using rocks to crack open shellfish

owl.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act and similar laws
owl.fun_fact: Owls cannot move their eyeballs so they rotate their entire head up to 270 degrees — they also have three eyelids

ox.fun_fact: Oxen were one of the earliest work animals — they have been used by humans for plowing and pulling loads for over 6,000 years

oyster.fun_fact: A single oyster can filter up to 190 litres of water per day making them crucial for maintaining clean coastal ecosystems

panda.protection_details: Protected under Chinese law and CITES Appendix I — symbol of WWF
panda.fun_fact: Giant pandas eat for up to 16 hours a day consuming 12-38 kg of bamboo because bamboo has almost no nutritional value

parrot.protection_details: Protected under CITES — many species listed in Appendix I or II
parrot.fun_fact: Parrots do not just mimic sounds — some species understand words in context and Alex the African Grey parrot had the intelligence of a 5-year-old child

pelecaniformes.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
pelecaniformes.fun_fact: Pelicans have a pouch that can hold up to 13 litres of water — three times more than their stomach capacity

penguin.protection_details: Protected under Antarctic Treaty and national laws
penguin.fun_fact: Penguins propose to their mates with a pebble — if the female accepts she adds it to their shared nest

pig.fun_fact: Pigs are among the most intelligent animals — they can play video games with joysticks and have better long-term memory than dogs

pigeon.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
pigeon.fun_fact: Pigeons were used to deliver messages in both World Wars — one pigeon named Cher Ami saved nearly 200 soldiers and was awarded the French Croix de Guerre

porcupine.fun_fact: Porcupine quills are actually modified hairs coated with keratin — they detach easily on contact but cannot be fired or thrown

possum.protection_details: Protected in Australia under wildlife protection laws
possum.fun_fact: Possums play dead so convincingly when threatened that predators lose interest — this involuntary response can last up to 4 hours

raccoon.fun_fact: Raccoons have extremely dexterous hands and can open locks, unscrew jars, and pick locks — their front paws have four times more sensory receptors when wet

rat.fun_fact: Rats laugh when tickled — they emit ultrasonic chirps that are inaudible to humans and they are highly empathetic animals

reindeer.fun_fact: Reindeer are the only mammals that can see ultraviolet light — this helps them spot predators and find food in Arctic snow

rhinoceros.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix I — poaching for horns is primary threat
rhinoceros.fun_fact: Rhino horns are made of keratin — the same material as human fingernails — not bone, yet they are poached for traditional medicine

sandpiper.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
sandpiper.fun_fact: Some sandpipers migrate over 30,000 km round trip annually — one of the longest migrations of any bird species

seahorse.habitat: Shallow tropical and temperate waters, seagrass beds
seahorse.fun_fact: Seahorses are the only species where the male becomes pregnant and gives birth — the male can carry up to 2,000 babies

seal.protection_details: Protected under Marine Mammal Protection Act
seal.fun_fact: Seals can hold their breath for up to 2 hours and dive to depths of 900 metres — their blood contains far more oxygen than human blood

shark.protection_details: Many species protected under CITES and national laws
shark.fun_fact: Sharks are older than trees — they have existed for 450 million years and survived all five mass extinctions on Earth

sheep.fun_fact: Sheep have excellent memories and can remember up to 50 individual sheep and 10 human faces for up to 2 years

regions: Worldwide except Ireland, Iceland, Greenland, Antarctica
snake.fun_fact: Snakes can sense heat through pit organs on their face allowing them to hunt warm-blooded prey in complete darkness

sparrow.protection_details: Protected under Wildlife and Countryside Act
sparrow.fun_fact: House sparrows were introduced to North America in 1851 and have since spread across the entire continent — one of the most successful birds ever

squid.fun_fact: The giant squid has the largest eyes of any living animal — up to 30 cm in diameter — to detect bioluminescent prey in the deep ocean

squirrel.fun_fact: Squirrels plant thousands of trees by forgetting where they buried their nuts — they are responsible for much of the world's forest regeneration

starfish.habitat: All ocean zones, tidal pools, coral reefs
starfish.fun_fact: Starfish have no brain and no blood — they use sea water as their circulatory system and can regenerate lost arms completely

swan.protection_details: All mute swans in UK are property of the Crown and legally protected
swan.fun_fact: Swans mate for life and can live for over 30 years — they are one of the few birds known to die of a broken heart after losing their partner

tiger.protection_details: Protected under CITES Appendix I — all trade banned internationally
tiger.fun_fact: No two tigers have the same stripe pattern — just like human fingerprints their stripes are completely unique to each individual

turkey.protection_details: Wild turkeys protected by hunting regulations
turkey.fun_fact: Turkeys can run at 40 km/h and fly at 88 km/h — Benjamin Franklin proposed the turkey as the US national bird instead of the eagle

turtle.protection_details: Most sea turtles protected under Endangered Species Act and CITES
turtle.fun_fact: Sea turtles navigate using the Earth's magnetic field and always return to the exact beach where they were born to lay their own eggs

whale.habitat: All oceans from surface to deep sea
whale.protection_details: Protected under International Whaling Commission moratorium since 1986
whale.fun_fact: The blue whale is the largest animal ever to have existed on Earth — its heart alone weighs as much as a car

wolf.protection_details: Protected under Endangered Species Act in parts of USA
wolf.fun_fact: Wolves howl not just to communicate but to locate pack members — a wolf can hear a howl from up to 16 km away

wombat.fun_fact: Wombats produce cube-shaped droppings — they are the only animal in the world to do this and use it to mark territory

regions: Worldwide except Australia and polar regions
woodpecker.protection_details: Protected under Migratory Bird Treaty Act
woodpecker.fun_fact: Woodpeckers peck up to 20 times per second and up to 12,000 times per day — their skull has special shock-absorbing structure to prevent brain damage

zebra.protection_details: Protected in national parks and game reserves across Africa
zebra.fun_fact: Zebra stripes are unique like fingerprints and recent research shows the stripes act as a natural insect repellent by confusing flies
